subject: Internal medicine
title: Chest pain
preambleMarkdown:
# Chest pain



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Chest pain is a symptom, syndrome, or diagnostic approach topic in internal medicine.
- This article focuses on adult presentation, differential diagnosis, diagnostics, complications, chronic management, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is separating common causes from time-sensitive dangerous causes.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Chest pain to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Chest pain is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is separating common causes from time-sensitive dangerous causes.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, progressive, or episodic presentation.
- Localized versus systemic syndrome.
- Stable outpatient problem versus emergency presentation.
- Primary disease, complication, medication effect, functional disorder, or psychosocial contributor.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Chest pain depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In internal medicine, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Chest pain.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Chest pain disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Chest pain should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Chest pain first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Chest pain, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define onset, timing, triggers, severity, associated symptoms, prior episodes, medication exposures, and functional impact.
- The same symptom can represent benign disease or a time-sensitive emergency.
- Vital signs and general appearance often decide whether broad testing or immediate stabilization comes first.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Chest pain, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused internal medicine examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Chest pain presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Chest pain with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Chest pain is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Chest pain should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize life-threatening causes, treat pain or distress, and choose tests that separate dangerous from common causes.
- Treat the identified cause rather than the symptom label alone.
- Arrange follow-up and safety-net instructions when initial evaluation is nondiagnostic.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Chest pain is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses separating common causes from time-sensitive dangerous causes.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Chest pain, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Chest pain may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Chest pain patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Chest pain, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Chest pain in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Chest pain, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Chest pain does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Chest pain may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Chest pain, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Chest pain as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- A symptom is not a diagnosis.
- First rule out unstable and cannot-miss causes.
- The next best test is the one that changes the next decision.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Chest pain draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Chest Pain

_Source lines: 4-91._

```text
Chest Pain
A. Differential Diagnosis
1. Heart, pericardium, vascular causes
a. Stable angina
b. ACS-unstable angina (UA), non-ST-elevation myocardial
infarction (NSTEMI), and ST-elevation myocardial infarction
(STEMI)
c. Pericarditis
d. Aortic dissection
2. Pulmonary: Pulmonary embolism (PE, can have pain with
pulmonary infarction), pneumothorax, pleuritis (pleural pain),
pneumonia, status asthmaticus
3. GI: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), diffuse esophageal
spasm, peptic ulcer disease, esophageal rupture
4. Chest wall: Costochondritis, muscle strain, rib fracture, herpes
zoster
5. Psychiatric: Panic attacks, anxiety, somatization
6. Cocaine/methamphetamine use can cause angina or MI
Quick HIT
If you suspect a cardiac cause of the pain, sublingual nitroglycerin is
appropriate. Also give aspirin if the patient does not have a bleeding
disorder.

B. Approach to Treating a Patient With Chest Pain
1. Rule out any life-threatening causes. These include ACS, aortic
dissection, pericarditis with cardiac tamponade, pulmonary

embolism, tension pneumothorax, and esophageal rupture
2. Assess vital signs
3. Develop a focused history
a. Character of the pain (pressure, squeezing, tearing, sharp,
stabbing, etc.)

b. Location of pain
c. Severity of pain
d. Duration of pain
e. Setting in which pain occurred (during exertion, at rest, after
meal)
f. Radiation of pain
g. Aggravating or alleviating factors (e.g., meal, exertion, rest,
respiration)
h. Does the patient have a cardiac history? Ask about results of
previous stress tests, echocardiograms, cardiac catheterization,
or of any procedures (PCI or CABG)
i. If the patient has a history of angina, ask how this episode differs
from previous ones (more severe? longer duration?)

4. Perform a focused physical examination, with attention to
cardiopulmonary, abdominal, and musculoskeletal examination
5. Order ancillary tests
a. Obtain ECG in almost all cases
b. Cardiac enzymes (e.g., troponin) depending on clinical
suspicion
c. Obtain chest x-ray (CXR) in almost all cases
d. Under appropriate clinical setting, work up the patient for PE
(see Pulmonary section)
6. Develop a diagnosis
a. It can be dificult to distinguish between GI causes of chest pain
and angina. The decision of whether to initiate a cardiac
workup is dependent on a patient's overall risk of coronary
artery disease (CAD) and the clinical presentation
b. There is no fail-proof algorithm for approaching chest pain. In
general, have a greater index of suspicion for ischemic causes in
the elderly, diabetic populations, and those with a history of
CAD (see myocardial infarction [MI] section). Risk stratification
scores can be helpful for evaluating patients in the emergency
department (e.g., HEART score)
Quick HIT
If nitroglycerin relieves the pain, a cardiac cause is more likely
(although nitrates also relieve pain from diffuse esophageal spasm).

Quick HIT
If chest pain is pleuritic, positional, or reproduced with chest wall
palpation, a cardiac cause may be less likely.

Quick HIT
A common presentation is a patient with chronic stable angina who
presents with symptoms suggestive of unstable angina. The
following are the initial steps:
- Obtain ECG and cardiac enzymes
- Give aspirin
- Begin IV heparin if no contraindications (i.e., active bleeding,
recent life-threatening bleed)
- Admission to hospital for workup as described above
```

### Source Excerpt: Chest Pain

_Source lines: 46022-46261._

```text
Chest Pain
A. Causes
1. Heart/vascular: Angina, MI, pericarditis, aortic dissection
2. GI: Gastroesophageal reflux disease, diffuse esophageal spasm,
esophageal tear or rupture, peptic ulcer disease, gallbladder
disease, acute cholecystitis
3. Chest wall: Costochondritis, rib fracture, muscle strain, herpes

zoster

## 4. Psychiatric: Anxiety, somatization

5. Pulmonary: PE, pneumothorax, pleuritis
6. Stimulant use: Can cause angina or MI
B. Management
1. As always, check vital signs. In most cases, obtain a 12-lead ECG.

Compare with an old ECG. Get more information about the
patient's cardiac history and current history of chest pain
(concerning for cardiac ischemia if worsens with exertion, radiates
to arms, or associated with diaphoresis, nausea, or vomiting).

2. Order ECG and cardiac enzymes (troponin)  3, every 4 to 8
hours, if acute coronary syndrome is suspected. Obtain ECG more
frequently (as often as every few minutes) if ongoing chest pain or
dynamic ECG changes.

3. Consider CXR (pneumothorax, widened mediastinum, pleural
effusion). Consider ABG or CT scan/scan if PE is suspected.

4. If acute coronary syndrome is suspected:
a. Supplemental oxygen if SpO 2 < 90%, titrate up as needed.

b. Nitroglycerin (sublingual) for pain; if pain continues, can give
opioids or nitroglycerin IV infusion until free of chest pain.

Nitroglycerin is contraindicated in suspected right ventricular
infarction or if patient took a PDE-5 inhibitor (such as
sildenafil) in last 24 hours.

c. Keep systolic BP >90 mm Hg.

d. Antiplatelet therapy
i. Aspirin
ii. P2Y 12 inhibitor (clopidogrel, prasugrel, ticagrelor, etc.)

e. Anticoagulant therapy
i. Heparin-give a loading dose, then start a drip. Check the
PTT at baseline and in 6 hours. Ensure there are no
contraindications to anticoagulation prior to starting (recent
intracranial hemorrhage, active bleeding).

f. High-intensity statin within first 24 hours
g. Put the patient on a cardiac monitor, and consider transfer to a
cardiac care unit or to a center capable of performing
percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI).

5. Treat other suspected conditions appropriately. See discussions of
PE, aortic dissection, pneumothorax, GERD, and PUD.

Tachycardia
A. Types: Determine whether the tachycardia is a narrow or wide

complex. Obtain an ECG-is it regular? If wide, treat like ventricular
tachycardia (VT). If narrow, determine whether the tachycardia is
sinus or non-sinus.

B. Sinus Tachycardia
1. HR >100, hardly ever >200 beats/min.

2. Sinus P waves precede each QRS complex and P waves are upright
in lead II.

3. Causes include pain, exercise, hypovolemia (dehydration or
bleeding), PE, hyperthyroidism, fever/inflammation, anemia,
anxiety, panic attacks, decongestants, beta-agonists, and substance use
or withdrawal.

4. Treat the underlying cause. In older patients with cardiac disease,
consider a beta-blocker to prevent the increase in myocardial O 2
demand that occurs at a high HR. Never start a beta-blocker to "treat
the tachycardia" unless you have ruled out all serious causes above.

C. Narrow Complex Non-Sinus Tachycardia
1. Also known as supraventricular tachycardias (SVT). May be due to
atrial flutter, atrial fibrillation, AV reentrant tachycardia (AVRT),
or AV nodal reentrant tachycardia (AVNRT).

2. Treatments vary according to the arrhythmia and presence of
instability.

a. If any unstable SVT (hypotension, altered mental status,
ischemic chest pain, or SOB), synchronized cardioversion is
recommended treatment.

b. AVRT or AVNRT-treat with vagal maneuvers or IV adenosine.

Prior to any treatment, ensure patient is on a cardiac monitor
and the crash cart is at bedside. AV nodal blockers may also be
used.

c. Atrial fibrillation-control the rate with a beta-blocker, DC
cardioversion, anticoagulation.

d. Atrial flutter-treat as with atrial fibrillation.

e. VT-if the patient is stable, give IV amiodarone. If the patient is
unstable, perform DC cardioversion.

D. Wide Complex Tachycardia
1. Either ventricular tachycardia (VT) or SVT with aberration (early
reentrant pathway)
2. Treat all wide complex tachycardias as VT unless proven otherwise.

The probability of a WCT being VT is even higher if the patient has
a history of ischemic heart disease. If patient is stable, give
antiarrhythmics such as IV amiodarone. If the patient is unstable,
perform immediate cardioversion.

3. Ventricular fibrillation-perform immediate defibrillation and CPR.

Oliguria
A. Typically Defined as Urine Output <500 mL/day or <0.3 mL/
kg/hour
B. Causes
1. Not all AKI is oliguric. Causes for oliguria mirror causes of AKI.

2. Prerenal: hypotension, hypovolemia, cardiorenal syndrome,
hepatorenal syndrome, renal arterial occlusion.

3. Renal: glomerulonephritis, acute tubular necrosis, acute interstitial
nephritis, vascular insult, and so on.

4. Postrenal: obstruction of lower or upper (bilateral) urinary tract.

C. Management
1. Check vital signs.

2. Inquire about potential precipitating factors-for example, recent
IV contrast administration (although it is controversial whether
contrast-induced nephropathy is as common as once thought) new
medications (NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, antibiotics), surgery, recent
intake and output, other comorbid conditions (e.g., sepsis, CHF).

3. Palpate the bladder, check a post-void residual, and consider
insertion of a Foley catheter if obstruction is suspected. If the
patient already has a Foley catheter, flush it with 20 to 30 mL of
saline to make sure it is not clogged. If urine flows after the Foley
placement or flushing, obstruction was most likely the cause.

4. If clinical syndrome or history is consistent with hypovolemia, trial
a bolus of IV crystalloid fluid (NS or LR) and monitor for
improvement.

5. If etiology remains unclear, order serum and urine chemistries and
a renal function panel. Calculate the fractional excretion of sodium
(FeNa <1% is consistent with prerenal causes). If the patient is on
diuretics, calculate the fractional excretion of urea (as the FeNa is
no longer reliable). Note that there are multiple etiologies of AKI
with FeNa <1%, so this is not always clear cut.

6. Consider a renal ultrasound to exclude hydronephrosis.

7. If CHF and cardiorenal syndrome is suspected, consider diuresis
(e.g., furosemide 20 to 60 mg IV).

8. Stop offending agents (nephrotoxic drugs).

9. Treat electrolyte disturbances and acid-base derangements, and
determine if there is an indication for dialysis (Table A-4).

TABLE A-4 Indications for Urgent Dialysis: "AEIOU"
A: Acidosis with pH <=7.1
E: Electrolyte derangements (hyperkalemia at any level with cardiac conduction
abnormalities, K >=6.5 mEq/L, or K >=5.5 mEq/L with ongoing tissue breakdown)
I: Intoxication (acute poisoning)
O: Overload (pulmonary edema)
U: Uremia (signs including pericarditis, otherwise unexplained decline in mental
status)
Fever in the Hospitalized Patient
A. Causes
1. Infection: have a systematic approach to consider the following:
a. Devices: central lines, peripheral IVs, Foley catheter, other
indwelling devices
b. Nosocomial infection: hospital-acquired pneumonia, UTI,
pressure wounds, surgical site infection, C. dificile colitis
c. Other infections: skin and soft tissue infection, pneumonia, UTI,
bacteremia, endocarditis, GI tract infection (e.g., acute
cholecystitis), septic arthritis, meningitis
2. Noninfectious causes: PE/DVT, medications (especially antibiotics),
neoplasms, connective tissue disorders, postoperative inflammatory
state.

B. Diagnosis
1. Check vital signs (tachycardia is an expected physiologic response
to fever). Screen for sepsis criteria (tachycardia, fever, elevated
WBC count, tachypnea) and treat accordingly.

2. Evaluate for signs and symptoms of localizing disease-for
example, cough, abdominal pain, dysuria, meningeal signs, joint
pain, diarrhea, rash, erythema, or drainage from wounds or
devices.

3. Consider CBC, CXR, cultures (blood from two different sites and
from any indwelling lines or ports, urinalysis and/or culture,
sputum, any fluid collections), with sensitivity panels.

4. Lumbar puncture if meningitis is a possible cause; CT of the
abdomen and pelvis if intra-abdominal infection is suspected.

C. Therapies

1. Acetaminophen is appropriate for symptomatic relief in most cases.

2. If the patient is ill-appearing, hemodynamically unstable or
neutropenic, start broad-spectrum antimicrobial treatment
empirically after cultures are obtained. Transfer to an ICU if patient
is in septic shock.

3. If the patient is on an antibiotic and fever recurs, consider adding
another antibiotic or changing the antibiotic altogether. Note that
fevers may recur in the first 24 hours of starting appropriate
antimicrobial therapy. Antifungal agents may be required.

4. Remove or replace indwelling IV lines and catheters. Send the tips
of central lines for bacterial and/or fungal culture.

5. If no signs of infection and patient is hemodynamically stable, treat
underlying etiology or continue diagnostic workup.
```

### Source Excerpt: Chest Pain

_Source lines: 2129-2372._

```text
I. CHEST PAIN
A. Discussion
1. Visceral pain. Visceral afferent fibers are present in the sympathetic and para-
sympathetic nerves of the chest. These nonmyelinated fibers provide sensation
from the heart, pericardium, lungs, and all visceral structures embryologically
derived from the foregut.

2. Somatic pain. Somatic innervation gives rise to sensation from the mesodermal
structures (e.g., the parietal pleura and peritoneum and muscular, skeletal, and
dermal structures).

3. Referred pain. Both visceral and somatic afferent fibers share synaptic projec-
tions in the spinal cord and brain stem, which can cause visceral pain signals
to be perceived in somatic structures. An example is the classic radiation of
cardiac pain to the arms, neck, teeth, or jaw.

4. "Atypical" chest pain is a term often used to refer to chest pain that does not
clearly fit in any category. The use of this term as shorthand for "nonsignificant
chest pain" should be avoided because many serious diseases present atypically.

B. Clinical features
1. History. The history is the most important tool in determining the cause of a
patient's pain.

a. Description of pain. A description of the pain with regard to its onset,
location, quality, duration, and radiation must be obtained.

i. The patient should be questioned regarding exacerbating and relieving
factors, such as activity, position, exertion, swallowing (solids versus
liquids), meals, respiration, and medications (especially analgesic and
antacid use).

ii. The distinction between visceral and somatic pain is clinically useful
but not well defined. Deep skeletal structures such as joint capsules
and vertebrae are innervated by poorly localized nonmyelinated fibers.

Conversely, disease processes in visceral organs can irritate adjacent
somatically innervated structures; for example, myocardial infarction
(MI) can cause pericardial irritation, leading to the typical sharp pain of
pericarditis.

b. Description of previous episodes. A history of previous episodes of chest
pain or trauma should be sought.

c. Determination of risk factors. Many significant causes of chest pain have
identifiable risk factors; therefore, the patient's family history, medical his-
tory, and social history (especially concerning the use of tobacco, cocaine,
or alcohol) may reveal clues pertinent to the current episode.

Quick 	HIT
Visceral pain signals are
poorly localized and per-
ceived as "dull" or "aching."

Cardiovascular Emergencies

## 2. Symptoms

a. Symptoms of acute, critical illness. Recognition of the following symptoms
mandates immediate intervention according to the ABC principle (airway,
breathing, and circulation; see Chapter 1) prior to further evaluation or
work-up.

i. Inability to speak
ii. Difficulty breathing
iii. Thready pulse
iv. Rapid or very slow heart rate
v. Systolic blood pressure less than 100 mm Hg
vi. Diaphoresis
vii. Confusion
b. Autonomic symptoms (e.g., nausea, vomiting, diaphoresis, tachypnea,
eructation) are often associated with visceral chest pain as a result of neural
connections in the brain stem and in autonomic ganglia.

c. Palpitations, dizziness, fever, coughing , or weakness may be seen, depend-
ing on the underlying cause of the chest pain.

C. Differential diagnoses
1. Life-threatening causes of chest pain
a. Myocardial ischemia. The signs and symptoms of MI and stable and
unstable angina are discussed in section II.

b. Pulmonary embolism causes a spectrum of symptoms ranging from no
chest pain at all to sharp, pleuritic, or dull chest pain (see Chapter 3.VI).

The dogma that pulmonary embolism always causes pleuritic chest pain is
based on studies of hospitalized patients, and even in this group, pleuritic
chest pain is described only 75% of the time.

c. Aortic dissection is discussed in XII.A.

d. Cardiac tamponade is discussed in IX.C.

e. Tension pneumothorax. Patients usually present with dyspnea accompanied
by the signs or symptoms of shock. In most cases, the complaint of pain
occurs in the context of a known history of trauma. Tension pneumothorax
evolving from spontaneous pneumothorax is a consideration in a patient
with respiratory distress and chest pain.

f. Acute esophageal perforation (Boerhaave syndrome) can cause sharp pleu-
ritic, poorly localized, midline pain anywhere from the base of the neck to
the epigastrium. Pain is not invariably present initially, but with the devel-
opment of mediastinitis over the following days, pain becomes more diffuse,
constant, and severe and is associated with systemic signs of infection.

2. Serious but not immediately life-threatening causes of chest pain
a. Stable angina is discussed in II.A.

b. Pneumothorax (see also Chapter 3.X) can be traumatic or spontaneous
(i.e., atraumatic). The former usually presents little diagnostic difficulty.

Spontaneous pneumothorax is often associated with minimal symptoms and
mild chest discomfort without significant dyspnea. In view of these nonspe-
cific clinical findings, the emergency physician should have a low threshold
for obtaining chest radiographs in patients with atypical chest discomfort.

c. Pneumonia is suggested by cough and fever with or without chest pain. The
pain is classically sharp, localized, and pleuritic in nature.

d. Abdominal processes. Diseases of the stomach, liver, gallbladder, spleen,
and kidneys can cause chest pain by one of several mechanisms.

i. Referred pain. Pain fibers from the gallbladder can lead to the percep-
tion of pain in the right scapular region, and pain fibers from the spleen
can lead to the perception of pain in the left scapular area. Painful pro-
cesses affecting the central diaphragm can be referred to the third and
fourth cervical dermatomes.

ii. Pain originating from structures in direct proximity to the diaphragm
can be perceived as originating in the chest. Sources of this type of pain
include subdiaphragmatic abscesses and perforated peptic ulcers.

26 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Cardiovascular Emergencies
iii. Extension of abdominal disease processes into the chest can lead to
chest pain. Examples include diaphragmatic herniation of abdominal
viscera and the development of pleural effusions or empyema secondary
to a hepatic or subdiaphragmatic abscess.

3. Chronic or benign causes of chest pain
a. Pericarditis is discussed in IX.A.

b. Mitral valve prolapse is discussed in VIII.B.3.

c. Esophageal diseases usually cause a poorly defined, burning, midsternal
chest pain that can often be reproduced by asking the patient to swallow
cold fluids or food. Pain of esophageal origin is referred in the same pattern
as that of the heart, making for diagnostic difficulties.

d. Musculoskeletal disorders. Patients describe well-localized pain that can
be reproduced by palpation or specific movements. Causes include muscle
strain, costosternal and costochondral inflammation, osteoarthritis of the
spine, and bursitis, arthritis, or tendinitis of the shoulder girdle. Such con-
ditions can coexist with serious causes of chest pain.

e. Pulmonary and abdominal processes of a less life-threatening nature in-
clude viral pneumonitis, pleurisy, nonperforating peptic ulcer, biliary dis-
ease, chronic pancreatitis, and most forms of hepatitis.

D. Evaluation. Most patients with chest pain should be placed on supplemental oxy-
gen and monitored using a pulse oximeter, cardiac monitor, and blood pressure
monitor until it is determined that they are not at risk for acute decompensation
and do not require emergent intervention.

1. Physical examination
a. As a part of the initial assessment of the ABCs, the vital signs should be
noted prior to obtaining the history.

b. Initial attention to the cardiopulmonary examination should be followed by
a comprehensive examination in all stable patients.

2. Diagnostic tests
a. Electrocardiography. The electrocardiogram (ECG) gives direct and indi-
rect information about many of the causes of chest pain. ECG should be
obtained in all patients in whom the history and physical examination have
not definitively ruled out a cardiopulmonary etiology. A normal ECG does
not exclude any cause of chest pain.

b. Radiography. A chest radiograph, like electrocardiography, is a rapid, non-
invasive, and inexpensive way of ruling out or identifying many of the
diseases in the differential diagnosis (e.g., pneumothorax, pneumonia,
congestive heart failure [CHF], and, indirectly, pulmonary embolus and
aortic dissection). A chest radiograph should be obtained unless the patient
clearly has a disorder for which it is noncontributory.

c. Arterial blood gas (ABG). An ABG is rarely diagnostic but can be used as a
measure of severity of illness or to assess acid-base status.

d. Pulse oximetry provides instant information about a patient's oxygenation
status.

e. Serum markers of myocardial injury
i. Cardiac troponins. The troponin complex is the main protein of the
thin filament of the myofibrils that regulate the calcium-dependent
adenosine triphosphate hydrolysis of actomyosin. Levels are reliably
elevated in myocardial ischemia in all patients at 6 hours, peak at
12 hours, and remain elevated for 7 to 10 days.

ii. Creatine kinase (CK), CK-MB subunit, and myoglobin. CK is an in-
tracellular enzyme involved in the transfer of high-energy phosphate
groups from adenosine triphosphate to creatine. CK-MB from cardiac
muscle becomes elevated within 4 to 8 hours after coronary artery oc-
clusion, peaks between 12 to 24 hours, and returns to normal between
3 and 4 days (Table 2-1).

f. Bedside echocardiography is diagnostic of cardiac tamponade and can
provide information about cardiac wall motion and valvular function.

Quick 	HIT
When there is doubt, assume
that the pain is cardiac in
origin and act accordingly
pending further work up.

C A R D I O V A S C U L A R E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	27

Cardiovascular Emergencies

E. Disposition

## 1. Admission

a. Patients with chest pain due to life-threatening causes require admission to
an intensive care unit (ICU) and in some instances will require emergent
cardiac catheterization or surgery. Early subspecialty consultation in the
emergency department (ED) is indicated.

b. Patients with serious but not life-threatening causes of chest pain will most
likely require admission to the hospital for further evaluation and treat-
ment. Discussion with the patient's primary physician provides additional
historical information and ensures coordinated outpatient follow-up for
those patients well enough for discharge.

2. Discharge. Patients with benign or chronic causes of chest pain can, in most
instances, be safely discharged with a prescription for analgesics and arrange-
ments for outpatient follow-up.
```
